speaker: interviewer
text: Today is November 5th, 1996. Sari Baron is being interviewed by Betsy Stern in Fairfield, Connecticut, United States, in English. This is tape number one. November 5th, 1996. Sari Baron is being interviewed by Betsy Stern in Fairfield, Connecticut, United States, in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is Sari Baron. Let's begin, Sari, with your telling us your maiden name and where you were born.

speaker: interviewee
text: My maiden name was Sari Stern. And I was born in Beled, Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that for us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-E-L-E-D, Beled.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in 1926, October 27.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Bertha Stern. Her maiden name was Hoffmann.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: H-O-F-F-M-A-N-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Joseph Stern. And I also had a brother and a sister. My brother's name was Alex Stern, and my sister's name was Lea Stern.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were younger than you? Your brother--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were younger. My brother was three years younger, and my sister was seven years young-- seven years younger.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of a town was Beled?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beled was a very small town, but we still had, I would think, about 120 Jewish families-- maybe between 120, 150. I'm not sure, I don't remember exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how big a town was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't remember any numbers or anything. It really wasn't considered as a very big town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father had a fruit place that he was selling fruit in wholesale. He also was selling other things in between, whatever he really came under. He had cars or horses, whatever, something on that. But basically, we were dealing with fruit.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the fruit came in from the towns around?

speaker: interviewee
text: From, different stands. And then he was delivering to smaller places.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother work with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she helped a lot. She did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember other family?

speaker: interviewee
text: From my hometown?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Families?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, family of yours that lived there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had cousins there. My uncle was there who had a bakery, Hoffmann. And we worked a lot in the bakery, helped out because he, at that time, wasn't home. He was in the army and he had his wife and the children. And I would go in the morning and help them to cut all the bread for the guys who were taking it or the rolls, so things like that. And I had other aunts in the town. And then I had my grandfather in the very same town, and an aunt. My grandmother already passed away, but I remembered her very well, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your family celebrate the holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were very close. We always celebrated close. We most all the time went to my grandfather's house. And we celebrated together. I never remember my father making Passover anything. My grandfather used to take care of all that thing. And we were a very happy family, basically.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get along with all the non-Jews. What was life like there?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a funny thing. My father was very friendly with the mayor, and he had actually told him that things are happening and he doesn't know what's going to happen. He should make sure that his family's being taking care of. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before, before 1939, though-- before 1940, what was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a very nice life. We were the average type of a family, and we had everything that we needed or we wanted. We weren't missing anything at all. And everybody was really happy. In 1942 already--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we get to '42, I have a few other questions.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to school in Beled.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went to regular public school. Then, for a year, I started to go to higher-up schools. But in this point, things weren't so good anymore, and I just wasn't happy in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would go to school that it wasn't in my hometown. And the boys and girls went with me who was non-Jewish. You see, before, I went to a Jewish school. All of us was Jewish because the city where I was, everybody went to their own school. The Catholic went to Catholic school. The Jews went to Jewish school. But at that point, I was mixed in with other children. And they would pick me up on the train traveling back and forth because I was a Jew and I wasn't as good as they were. So I felt like, I don't want to go to school anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you the only Jewish child traveling with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, I wasn't. There were a couple of them before me. And eventually, they all dropped out. But I think I was one of the first ones who stopped going.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was-- my mother was pleading with me, and he-- and she said, you have to learn. It's very important in life. But I just wasn't much willing. And after struggle, OK, then you have to learn a trade. So I went to a school to do dress-making or pattern-making which was-- what would they call it over here? It's-- I don't even know-- designing school. Now, this was not just a Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this one wasn't, but it wasn't that much problem. It was in my own hometown, and it was no problem to do. And so I learned dress-making, which, in the end, it was very good because I used it a lot in between.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went to the school outside of your town--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't even remember the dates. I'm very, very poor on the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it when the Germans and the Hungarians were allies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I guess they started to be. The Germans weren't there yet. The Germans came afterwards. But things weren't already like it used to be. You could feel it that you were a different kind of religion.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you didn't feel that when you were younger?

speaker: interviewee
text: How much younger? No.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you and your family become aware that things were starting to change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when my father had to go in, and he was in the-- he was no longer in the army. He was like a [NON-ENGLISH], which is like he wasn't good to be in the army. So they put him into a working camp, whatever that--

speaker: interviewer
text: He wasn't good to be in the army because he was Jewish or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, because he was Jewish. So in that time, things started to be bad because my mother was home alone. She had to take of us. She had to take care of the business, and things became much harder. My grandfather started to get a little bit more ill, so it was a problem with that, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we're talking the early 1940s at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think it was more like in '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: In '42. And what kind of a war camp was your father sent to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't even know if they were really working there. They were just in the camp. I imagine they did something. I should know what they did because I was taking food for him every single day on a bicycle, which was 10 kilometers away from my home because he was only eating kosher food. And sometime, like Fridays, I would go twice because I was bringing food for the Shabbat, too. So I should remember what they did there, but somehow I just don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no trouble bringing food to the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The-- No, the name was [NON-ENGLISH], and it was like 10 kilometers away from us. And actually, it was just like on the field that had some barracks put up for them. I don't remember knowing what he did or what he didn't do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your mother very worried about what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was always worried. She really had to worry a lot because she was taking good care of us. And she was always trying to hide things from us. She never showed how difficult things become. And it was not easy at that point in time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you and your friends talk about what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Between ourselves?

speaker: interviewer
text: Between yourselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean my girlfriends or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't really think we discussed it too much. Everybody went pretty much in their own ways. We would get together, like on a Sabbath afternoon and like on holidays. But we still went on as children. We didn't pay too much attention what was really bothering our parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father stayed in the work camp until when?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, my father was staying there all through ghetto. But my grandfather got very ill and they had to take him away to a hospital, to a different city. And somehow, through the mayor, who was a good friend of him originally, managed to get us out of the ghetto and take us to Szombathely so they wouldn't know that my father is with us. That's one of the reasons we went away from our own town.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, well, let's talk about-- when did the ghetto start?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto start in on Shovuos time on 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we're talking May or June of 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, someplace around then. As I said, I'm very bad on--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any idea that, that things were going to change? That there was going to be a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I had no idea, but in Passover time, when we were getting our new clothes-- because we got clothes twice a year, for Passover and for Rosh Hashanah-- my father had taken us to the shoemaker and all three of us were fitted with shoes, which was customary in Europe. And then, when the shoes came home, my father was explaining it to us, if anything happens in one point or the other and we will be separated or even just overnight-- he didn't want it to scare us-- he said, not to worry, we have all the addresses in our shoes, between the soles. So if any time, we would get into a different country or something will come up, we should not worry that we're not going to see each other anymore. That will be something there where we could write, and he will write to the same place and they'll bring us together. And that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the first time that he--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the first time when I started to get scared that something is going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you said your father took you to the shoemaker. Was he able to leave the work camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were able to come home in a certain time, for, like, for a half a day. And that was before Passover, I remember that very well. And he came home, and I don't even think he came home after that until he came home on somehow-- somebody just brought him to the other ghetto, not even to the local place, where we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's talk about, then, when the ghetto around Shovuos How did you hear that there was going to be a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think Shovuos already, we, we were in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, actually, what happened to us in the ghetto was like that they took to get a bunch of Jewish homes that was close by, that lived next to each other, and they made it a ghetto. It wasn't that anything was closed-up or anything like that. It was just maybe five, six, or seven houses in one side, seven houses on the other side of the street. Where there was Jewish families, they put us together. So instead of one family to live in that place, maybe it was 15 families in that. But, you know, they'd just throw us together. And that was basically the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who guarded the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't much guarded. But I guess everybody guarded because the place was so small, the town, that they know that Jews shouldn't be out. Or if they were out, then somebody would come after us and take care of us somehow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that happening ever?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember any of that really happened, that we got into trouble. But we were so close to the police station that within a small distance, they would know if it's something. We were ordered not go out after 5 o'clock to the streets. So we had to stay in our places.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- your family had to move?

speaker: interviewee
text: We moved. We moved. We moved into a small room for-- from somebody else's bedroom was made a room for us. And this is basically what happened to all of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get food?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a difficult thing. Whatever we took with us, that's all what we had. That's what we had. Most of our people, at home, they had flour and sugar in sackloads, you know? So we took-- until we had it, we had it. But the truth that we had it till the last minute, until we ran away from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now tell us how you left the ghetto in Beled.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you leave the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: How did we leave the ghetto? Very badly. When we were leaving the ghetto, before we were leaving to go to the other ghettos, we were cleaned out by-- we had to go to a schoolyard. And they took all our belongings. And unfortunately, my case was even more difficult because the person who took my belonging was-- actually, this wasn't when we went away from the ghetto. From my house to go into the ghetto, it was a cousin of ours who turned Catholic short time before because they were afraid they were going to take him away, so they changed religion. And that cousin of mine is the one who pulled up my little ring that I got from my grandmother. And she said, you can't take it with you. And it was hurting me so much and I cried so much because it was my own cousin who we were close with-- all of a sudden, she'd become against us. And whatever I had asked, she took it off from me. And it was hurting a lot. But this kind of thing happened. But even my mother and my little sister and brother, everybody was examined from top to toe. We shouldn't hide anything. And everything was taken away from us, whatever we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hungarians examined you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hungarians. But this one, mine was a Jewish-- I mean, she turned non-Jewish. She was family. But most of the time, non-Jews who did that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there German soldiers there as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in that point. That was our own town people who we know very well. And it was very difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then how did you leave the ghetto in Beled?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was hard because we left somehow-- somebody took us out from the ghetto. And they closed our mouth and eyes because we were young. We shouldn't talk, because we were going into Szombathely. So my father was there already waiting for us-- and they shouldn't find out that we disappeared from the ghetto somehow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was the mayor who arranged this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was not supposed to be told. It was, but I think because he was so close with my father that he was the one. Because when I went back to Hungary, when he saw me, he said, listen, your father and mother left a little jewelry here and you come pick it up. So I assume, whatever information came, it came from him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you got to the second ghetto, your grandfather came with--

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather was in a different [NON-ENGLISH] in [NON-ENGLISH] because from Beled, they took everybody to Sopron, to another ghetto. And we were in Szombathely because they don't want him to know that we left. So we were in Szombathely. But somehow, from Szombathely, they had a chance to go to Sopron. And then we went to Sopron, hoping that we're going to find our grandfather there. And my grandfather was there, but we never saw him because he was still between the hospital group that they took from [NON-ENGLISH]. And, and they don't let us go see anybody. So we didn't see him. The only time I really saw my grandfather when we got to Auschwitz-Birkenau. Over there--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's wait til we get there.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now you're in the third ghetto. Did your father join you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was with us, from Szombathely to Sopron.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in that ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything was in short time. But I never know dates. I don't remember dates. And that's what I could tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember whether you were aware of what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really, exactly. We wasn't told everything. And I don't even know if my parents know everything, you know? It's-- we were all hoping that it was just temporary. I remember at one point, the two German men came and they said, well, if you want to stay together as family, you come forward and come with me. And my father said, no. We're going to go where all the Jews go. So I don't know what happened to the other group, if they really stayed together. Nobody that I know had come back as a family. So we didn't go, and we just went the way like all of them went.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you living in the ghetto? Did they just have a few Jewish houses that they kept?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the other ghettos already was different. They were all basically on a field. One was in a place where they were making blocks-- not cinder-blocks, the other ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bricks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bricks. So it was a brick factory and that's where they threw all of us. We didn't have nothing there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you guarded by Germans at that--

speaker: interviewee
text: There were guards. It's different kind of guards, but I don't think they were Germans. I think they were still Hungarians. They had Germans around, but I think per se they were all Hungarians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little. Very little. I think we only had bread and I don't know. It was very little. They made little ovens from the bricks outside, and whatever little things we had, my mother made with flour and water. I don't even know. It was like nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any clothes or anything with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whatever we took with us. It was very little belonging because we could all carry it in our hands. And that's all we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did they transport you from place to place?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the cattle cars. That's, that's the way it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to go to the rail station and filled in. And we travel about 50, 60 people into a cattle car. And that's how we went from Szombathely to Sopron. And from Sopron, we went to Germany, to [NON-ENGLISH]. We had babies born there. We had people dying there. And people got crazy. They were screaming all the time. Everybody was all over. No food. Not even water-- when be stopped, everybody was begging for a little water and we couldn't get nothing. And that was for two days, we were in the thing because at one point, they were going to take us through [NON-ENGLISH] to go to Germany. And by that time, they borders sort of were closed, so now they had to take us back to Vienna. We were right near Vienna-- Sopron is like very close by to Vienna. But they tried to circle us around to stay longer in the cattle cars and hopefully die. And then finally, we got to, to the Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your family was still together at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we never saw grandfather, but we-- we were together. It was my aunt-- my father's single sister-- my mother, and my father and my sister and brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when you got to Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we got to Birkenau and somebody told us that the hospital group where my grandfather is, it's the fourth cattle car down. And I started to look there to look-- to see--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you allowed to walk off the cattle car?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was so many people that you couldn't walk too far. But I just wanted to see my grandfather. And as they were telling us where the car is where my grandfather is, I saw them turning them onto an open truck. They were throwing all the people from the car to the truck. And I got-- I started to cry. And while I was turning down and crying, I lost my parents and everybody. By time I came to myself to look for them, my father and my brother and my sister, everybody was gone. And then I remember my mother had a new coat, so I went back to the car to take out her new coat to take with me. And I was hoping someplace we're going to meet yet. And of course that had never happened. And then somebody who was there already for a long time, they said, throw me the coat because you're not going to have it anyhow. Because they're taking everything away from you. But you know, I didn't realize that is true. And I hold onto my mother's fur coat until the last minute. And then we had to walk through Mengele's. You know, Mengele's was the one you go right or you go left. And that's where it happened-- I went to one side and I was looking for my mother, but I had never come across with, with her or even anybody else.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, at night, when we were there the first night and the girls were telling us who was there already for a long time. And I was crying, looking for my mother. So she said, why are you looking for your mother? You see the flame over there and the smoke? That's where your mother is. And that was the end of the-- Did you believe her?

speaker: interviewee
text: What can I tell you? That you don't want to believe anything because you think that telling your stories about then-- as days went by, you suddenly realize when you saw that things really was happening and that was the true story. And then they put us into a, a room that we must have been 50 girls sleeping on the floor. There was no roof or anything. If somebody had to go to the bathroom, which was no bathroom-- it was like an [? awning ?] little thing-- they went to on you because there was no way to get out. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: They climbed over you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all that it was, so-- it was-- I should stop, right?
